decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing / enterprise solutions, kitchen and bath products, wellness, hospitality, digital innovation
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget or contract value stated. Inferred potential opportunity size: approximately $5M-$15M+ for evaluated hosting/transition scenarios, based on enterprise-scale Wisconsin data center exit assessment covering VMware/VxRail, significant physical servers, SAP ecosystem, Windows/Linux estate, databases, backup/DR, networking, security tooling, and potential Cloud/DCaaS/Hybrid hosting. Phase 0 itself is an evaluation/business-case exercise and does not constitute a commitment to award.
decomposition.contract_duration: No explicit contract duration stated. Documented process is a Phase 0 evaluation running from RFP publication on 29 May 2026 through supplier presentations week of 6 July 2026; any subsequent hosting/implementation contract term is TBD and to be finalized after vendor selection. Inferred planning horizon for the hosting business case is likely multi-year, approximately 3-5 years, but this is not specified in the RFP.

requirements.text: Vendors must articulate a target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must cover landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must address environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must address identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must address hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must address region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears only as an architecture bullet and no explicit location restriction is stated, but failure to address it would leave a compliance gap.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required; no specific country/state residency mandate stated.

requirements.text: Vendors must address disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, including Cloud, DCaaS and Hybrid where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Current core hosting environment is Wisconsin-based.

requirements.text: In-scope services include commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications.
In-scope services include Windows and Linux virtual machines.
In-scope services include databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL and other supported platforms.
In-scope services include middleware and integration platforms.
In-scope services include file storage systems and shared infrastructure services.
In-scope services include backup and disaster recovery solutions.
In-scope services include identity and access management integrations.
In-scope services include network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments.
In-scope services include monitoring, logging and security tooling.
In-scope services include selected OT-adjacent workloads, limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workloads are included only in limited read-only or controlled integration scenarios; bidders proposing broader OT execution may mis-scope the solution.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset and use it as the baseline for sizing, solution design and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory spreadsheet is external to the main RFP and is mandatory as the baseline for sizing, solution design and indicative pricing.

requirements.text: Vendors must highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory certification evidence requirement is in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements subsection and references the response template for more detail; it can be missed if only the main scope is reviewed.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are required, but specific jurisdictions/standards are not named in the main RFP text.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required for applicable industry/regional standards.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Conditional mandatory requirement applies specifically to DCaaS providers and points to the Vendor Response Template for more information.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; RFP responses/proposals are due to Kohler on 26 June.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A bid submitted after the deadline or through the wrong communication route may not be accepted before evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory use of the external Vendor Response Template is easy to miss; simple Yes/No answers are expressly insufficient.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the Q&A template provided to the contact.
All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: All RFP communication is directed to a named contact and copy mailbox; failure to include the copy address may create process risk.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The Vendor Response Template is provided separately; not using it could make a proposal non-comparable.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the RFP requirements and inventory baseline.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and consulting, support or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: SAP cost separation is mandatory and can materially affect comparability of bids.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency requirement is buried in pricing guidelines and affects all commercial submissions.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
Payment terms are important; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and proposals should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 payment terms are a strong commercial gate even though the text says not competitive rather than expressly rejected.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the proposed solution.
Governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework, and high-level key roles and responsibilities.
All information shared as part of this RFP is confidential and must be treated accordingly by participating vendors.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality restrictions are in Appendix 8.1, a common place for missed legal disqualifiers.

requirements.text: Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Use restriction in appendix may affect internal reuse, partner handling, and proposal support processes.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This appendix clause may block sharing with subcontractors, affiliates, offshore teams, or proposal tooling unless prior written consent is obtained.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Broad compliance capability requirement appears in the legal/compliance appendix and may create downstream obligations not detailed in the main scope.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.

requirements.text: Kohler reserves the right to modify, amend, or withdraw this RFP at any stage of the process, request additional information or clarification, and accept or reject any proposal without obligation or liability.
Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning and execution activities, are out of scope for this Phase 0 RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Execution scope is explicitly excluded; vendors must avoid pricing or committing to execution as if included unless clearly identified.

requirements.text: Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities such as code changes, re-platforming and re-architecting are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Application remediation and modernization are excluded; vendors proposing refactoring as inherent scope may be misaligned.

requirements.text: Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope for this Phase 0 RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Steady-state managed services delivery commitments are excluded from the current scope even though pricing guidelines request managed services costs if applicable.

Mandatory response template risk: 'Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP' and 'Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.' Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent. [PAGE 11]
All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars. [PAGE 9]

similar_deals.title: Kohler Co. Project Polaris Hosting Options / Wisconsin Data Center Exit
similar_deals.industry: Manufacturing / enterprise solutions / hospitality / digital innovation
similar_deals.deal_size_usd: Phase 0 anchors found at $279,533 and $595,000; optional downstream hybrid modernization anchor $30,667,392; no explicit RFP budget
similar_deals.why_relevant: This is the exact same RFP profile: Wisconsin Data Center exit evaluation across cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid for SAP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, Windows/Linux, databases, backup/DR, networking, IAM, monitoring/logging, security, and manufacturing integrations.
similar_deals.key_lesson: The reusable strategy is a 12-week vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab with workload placement scorecards, landing-zone blueprints, open-book USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, DR/security/observability designs, migration waves, rollback gates, and vendor-coexistence RACI.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout
similar_deals.why_relevant: Manufacturing client across Germany, USA, and Mexico with large-scale SAP transformation and global delivery complexity. Relevant to Kohler’s SAP ecosystem, global footprint, and need for low-disruption transition planning.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Executive sponsor alignment and named reference quality mattered; a strong BMW case study and prior CTO relationship with the SAP lead were decisive.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to enterprise IT transformation, infrastructure/operating-model change, and manufacturing-sector credibility. Supports a rapid Phase 0 mobilisation theme for Polaris.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Fast mobilisation and sector credibility won the deal; team readiness within two weeks was explicitly cited as the win reason.

similar_deals.title: Healthcare IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Different industry but USA-based, similar enterprise transformation governance expectations, and close to the inferred $5M-$15M+ opportunity band.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Local legal/operating presence and pre-existing relationship were decisive. For Kohler, demonstrate US delivery presence, local accountability, and onsite Wisconsin engagement early.

similar_deals.title: Supply Chain AI Platform
similar_deals.why_relevant: Multi-region enterprise platform modernization with SAP and Oracle connectors. Relevant to Kohler’s manufacturing-adjacent digital innovation, integration, and application dependency complexity.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Proprietary accelerators and pre-built integrations beat lower hourly rates. Package landing-zone, SAP, Oracle, VMware/VxRail, migration, and hybrid-connectivity accelerators into a named framework.

similar_deals.title: Healthcare IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Comparable size and transformation scope, but different industry/geography. Most relevant as a warning that mandatory compliance failures can override technical merit.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Mandatory eligibility requirements can eliminate an otherwise strong proposal. Compliance matrix, certifications, submission rules, and staffing/legal conditions must be closed before bidding.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Honest estimated win probability is 42%. Positive evidence: the past-proposal corpus contains an exact-match Kohler Polaris WIP with strong reusable positioning, pricing anchors, and solution structure; the deal corpus shows relevant wins in manufacturing SAP/global transformation (DEAL-003), manufacturing IT transformation (DEAL-089), and US enterprise IT transformation near the inferred size band (DEAL-041). These support credible domain fit, rapid mobilisation, architecture-led delivery, and local accountability. Negative evidence: there is no public procurement award data to validate competitor pricing or incumbent win patterns, so no market-grounded competitor advantage can be assumed; the exact Kohler proposal is WIP/outcome unknown, not a proven win; Phase 0 is only an advisory/business-case step with no downstream award commitment; mandatory compliance, certification, DCaaS attestation, template, submission-channel, and USD-pricing requirements create real elimination risk; and the current excerpts do not yet evidence complete Kohler-specific Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity-provider integration, or hybrid-connectivity designs. Confidence is medium-low because analysis uses strong past-proposal and deal-corpus evidence but lacks the third validation layer of public procurement data.
capability_gaps: Environment segregation is a MUST_HAVE requirement for Prod/Test/Dev, but the past-proposal excerpts do not show an explicit Kohler-specific Prod/Test/Dev segregation design; this must be added with account/subscription, IAM, network, data, backup, logging, and change-control separation.

Compliance certification evidence is a mandatory RFP risk: the RFP requires evidence of applicable industry/regional certifications, but the intelligence provided does not include the actual certification pack to attach; absence could make the bid non-compliant.

If proposing DCaaS, the RFP requires explicit compliance attestations for DCaaS providers; the current intelligence contains the concept of a compliance-attestation matrix but not named DCaaS provider attestations.

Identity integration is a MUST_HAVE, but the response must be tied to Kohler’s actual enterprise identity providers and privileged-access model; the current intelligence only references IAM baselining and identity-first design, not confirmed Kohler IdP specifics.

Hybrid connectivity is a MUST_HAVE, but the response must include a concrete Wisconsin Data Center, plant/edge, cloud, DCaaS, and global-region connectivity model; the current intelligence references network-path baselining but does not yet evidence a final Kohler-specific connectivity architecture.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab: position Polaris as a defensible executive decision package, not a cloud-migration sales pitch, comparing public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration using workload placement scorecards and open-book USD TCO.

Manufacturing and SAP resilience: use DEAL-003 and DEAL-089 evidence to emphasize global manufacturing transformation, SAP ecosystem understanding, zero-disruption planning, and operational continuity for plants, hospitality, digital channels, and enterprise platforms.

Architecture-first, implementation-ready blueprint: lead with landing-zone, identity, network, Prod/Test/Dev segmentation, HA/DR, security, observability, FinOps, data residency, and migration-wave reference designs that Kohler can use with any downstream provider.

Rapid mobilisation with local accountability: commit a named US-led team, onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell, structured 12-week workshop calendar, and two-week mobilisation readiness, reflecting win patterns from DEAL-089 and DEAL-041.

Open-book economics and risk controls: provide USD-only scenario economics for cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid over a 3-5 year planning horizon, with assumptions, sensitivity analysis, rollback gates, vendor-coexistence RACI, and optional fee-at-risk tied to no evaluation-caused disruption.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so competitor win-rate and market price validation cannot be grounded in award data; this reduces confidence and prevents assuming a strong competitive position.
The strongest past-proposal intelligence is from the same Kohler Polaris WIP pursuit, not a proven win; it proves fit and reusable content but not buyer preference or win evidence.
Phase 0 is only an evaluation/business-case exercise and does not commit Kohler to a downstream hosting or implementation award, limiting near-term revenue certainty.

Potential incumbent or existing-provider relationship risk is unresolved. The past proposal references a vendor-coexistence RACI and Cognizant/vendor coexistence model, suggesting incumbent-provider complexity that could affect access, credibility, and downstream positioning.

Mandatory compliance failures could eliminate the bid regardless of technical quality, as shown by DEAL-024 and DEAL-008, where bids were lost due to mandatory conditions despite otherwise relevant capability.

Commercial ambiguity is a risk because past Polaris records show multiple price anchors: $279,533, $595,000, and $30,667,392 downstream. The proposal must clearly separate Phase 0 advisory pricing from optional implementation/managed-services scenarios.
Tight RFP timeline and designated submission channel create procedural risk; late, misrouted, non-template, non-USD, or incomplete submissions may be rejected before scoring.
must_fix_before_bidding: Build a mandatory compliance matrix against all 50 requirements and disqualifiers, including template usage, USD-only pricing, submission channel/date, confidentiality controls, certification evidence, and DCaaS attestations if applicable.

Choose and justify a Phase 0 commercial model. The most defensible anchors from past Polaris proposal content are $279,533 for a lean 12-week Hosting Decision Lab or $595,000 for a more robust three-month Control Tower; avoid confusing Kohler with multiple unpositioned prices.

Add explicit architecture diagrams/reference designs covering multi-account or multi-subscription landing zone, application deployment model, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, HA/DR, backup, monitoring/logging, and security controls.
Secure named US-based delivery leadership, Wisconsin onsite workshop coverage, and rapid mobilisation plan within two weeks, reflecting lessons from DEAL-089 and DEAL-041.

Prepare certification and attestation evidence pack before submission, including cloud/security certifications, DCaaS provider attestations if used, data residency approach, shared-responsibility model, and confidentiality controls for any subcontractors, affiliates, or tools.

client_intel.recommended_narrative: Position the proposal as a pragmatic hybrid-hosting decision framework, not a generic cloud migration. Emphasize app dependency mapping, TCO/FinOps, security-by-design, observability and data/AI readiness. Show how the work complements Kohler’s Cognizant ecosystem while giving Kohler objective hosting options and governance.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: high; confidence: medium-high. Evidence: Accenture has active U.S. hiring for SAP S/4HANA, SAP technology architecture, cloud migration, SAP Basis/infrastructure/middleware skills, and Wisconsin-related LCA/job material in Racine, WI; high is justified by U.S./Wisconsin hiring signals, though no named Kohler or Wisconsin hosting award was found [job posting intelligence/web search: Accenture careers and Racine WI LCA].
competitors.predicted_positioning: Accenture will likely position as the global 'reinvention partner' for a business-led data-center-exit and digital-core modernization: SAP, cloud, security, AI, manufacturing/Industry X, IT/OT, and global delivery. Evidence: Q3 FY26 materials cited 'Reinvention Partner' messaging and activity around SAP, manufacturing with NVIDIA, and federal services; Accenture announced the Industries eXcellence Group acquisition for Siemens Digital Industries capabilities and Microsoft/Avanade agentic factory work in 2026 [web search: Accenture Q3 FY26 investor materials; Accenture June 17 2026 acquisition release; Accenture April 20 2026 Microsoft/Avanade release].
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Downstream hosting/transition scenario: likely $8.0M-$15.0M; confidence: medium because no public Kohler/Phase 0 contract value exists. Calculation anchor: annualized revenue/employee = Q3 FY26 revenue $18.7B*4 / 799,000 employees = ~$93.6K per employee; operating margin = 17.0%; pricing floor for a $5M-$15M delivery cost base = cost/(1-0.17) = $6.02M-$18.07M. Accenture can credibly bid across the full inferred opportunity range but is unlikely to be the low-price floor bidder given its premium consulting positioning [SEC/web financial signal: Accenture Q3 FY26 results and fact sheet].
their_strengths: Financially strong and large-deal capable: Q3 FY26 revenue $18.7B, new bookings $19.3B, operating margin 17.0%, and ~799,000 employees [SEC/web financial signal: Accenture Q3 FY26 results/fact sheet].

Strong fit for SAP/cloud/manufacturing modernization: public sources cite SAP migration experience involving nine large data centers and 9,000+ SAP customers, plus 2026 manufacturing/Siemens and Microsoft agentic factory investments [web search: Accenture SAP case study; Accenture June/April 2026 releases].
Global delivery footprint: Accenture states it serves ~9,000 clients in 120+ countries with offices/operations in 52 countries and 200+ cities [web search: Accenture fact sheet].
their_weaknesses: No public source confirms a named Accenture-Kohler data-center-exit, hosting, or Phase 0 award; no recent contract wins in Wisconsin for this target were found in supplied sources [web search: Accenture snapshot].

Some growth pressure exists in public outlook: Accenture FY26 guidance references only 3%-4% local-currency revenue growth and an estimated 1% impact from U.S. federal business [SEC/web financial signal: Accenture FY26 outlook].

Their likely proposal may be broad and transformation-heavy; the RFP is a Phase 0 evaluation/business-case exercise with strict template, compliance-certification, USD-pricing, and submission-channel requirements, so over-positioning without detailed assumptions can be attacked [RFP pages 7-11].

cost_drivers.category: People / managed services: 16 FTE blended operations, architecture, SRE, DBA, security, network, FinOps and service-delivery team for 36 months
Infrastructure: API-priced AWS/Azure compute, database, storage and transfer scaled to enterprise hybrid estate, plus backup, DR, network, security, licensing and DCaaS integration uplift
Migration / transformation: includes $279,533 Phase 0 baseline, dependency mapping, target architecture, pilot waves, test/validation, transition runbooks and rollback gates
Governance / tooling: ITSM, FinOps, reporting dashboards, security/compliance evidence pack, architecture governance, vendor coexistence RACI and service management setup
Contingency: 12% of direct cost for dependency uncertainty, unknown licensing, DR sizing, SAP/database complexity and connectivity risk
Margin: HCLTech realistic gross margin on full 36-month program

pricing.competitive_rationale: Recommended Option B is anchored to HCLTech past Kohler Project Polaris pricing: $279,533 for the 12-week Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab, $595,000 for a higher-touch 3-month lab, and $15M-$50M+ / $30.7M broader downstream modernization references. Because the RFP term is not explicit, pricing assumes a 36-month full program covering Phase 0 evaluation, target-state architecture, migration/transformation waves, hybrid hosting enablement, and managed services. No public contract award values were supplied, so market validation is moderated; the recommended price is kept inside the competitor price-to-win band of $6.5M-$10.5M and aligned to the stated $5M-$15M+ opportunity sanity check. Live AWS/Azure unit prices are scaled to an enterprise estate rather than a single VM: Option B infrastructure includes 70 AWS app VMs using m5.xlarge at $0.192/hr, m5.2xlarge at $0.384/hr and r5.xlarge at $0.252/hr; RDS PostgreSQL using db.m5.xlarge at $0.462/hr, db.m5.2xlarge at $0.924/hr and db.r5.xlarge at $0.578/hr; Azure MySQL E8d_v5 at $0.945/hr and E16d_v5 at $1.890/hr; S3 storage at $0.023/GB-month; RDS gp3 at $0.115/GB-month; and data transfer at $0.09/GB. Raw API-priced cloud for Option B is approximately $1.56M over 36 months, uplifted to $2.135M for backup, DR, security tooling, network, licensing and hybrid/DCaaS integration. People cost assumes 16 FTEs at an offshore-heavy blended $42/hr for 36 months, appropriate for an HCLTech balanced delivery model. Math for recommended Option B: People $4.180M + Infrastructure $2.135M + Migration/Transformation $1.050M + Governance/Tooling $0.525M = $7.890M direct cost; +12% contingency $0.947M = $8.837M cost; +15.4% margin $1.613M = $10.450M total.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Treat this as a complex advisory-led infrastructure modernization pursuit, not just a hosting bid. Win by giving Kohler a defensible, neutral comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options with strong compliance evidence, transparent economics, and a low-risk transition path for SAP, VMware, databases, manufacturing connectivity, and DR-critical workloads. The proposal should demonstrate that we can execute the future migration/hosting program, while keeping Phase 0 objective and business-case driven.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Win by being the most credible neutral advisor: provide Kohler with a transparent, evidence-backed Cloud/DCaaS/Hybrid decision framework and business case that reduces data center exit risk while protecting SAP, manufacturing connectivity, security, compliance, and operational continuity.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid pricing strategy: competitive and transparent for Phase 0, value-based for the downstream roadmap, and scenario-based for hosting options. Price the evaluation phase as a fixed-fee or capped-fee assessment with clearly defined deliverables, assumptions, and optional add-ons, while presenting 3-5 year TCO ranges for Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid scenarios. Emphasize commercial transparency over lowest price: show unit rates, migration assumptions, managed-services costs, cloud consumption drivers, DR tiers, licensing treatment, and sensitivity ranges; quote everything in USD and accommodate net 90 if commercially feasible.
key_risks: The missing Evaluation Template may contain hidden weightings, mandatory requirements, pricing structure, or compliance criteria that materially change the pursuit strategy.
The missing inventory baseline creates sizing, costing, licensing, migration-wave, and DR-design uncertainty; assumptions must be explicit and commercially protected.

Kohler may be using Phase 0 to compare options without a firm implementation commitment, so the proposal must win the advisory/business-case phase while positioning for downstream migration and hosting work.
Competitors may underprice Phase 0 to secure the larger transformation/managed-services opportunity; pricing must be competitive without giving away high-value assessment work.
Compliance evidence is a gating risk, especially if proposing DCaaS or using third-party data centers, hyperscalers, subcontractors, or offshore delivery resources.
Net 90 payment terms may affect cash flow and margin; anything less favorable may be scored poorly even if not formally eliminatory.
The client appears to want neutral comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid; an overly cloud-biased or single-platform proposal could lose credibility.
SAP HANA, Oracle, database licensing, VMware licensing changes, backup/DR, network egress, and cloud consumption variability could create major TCO surprises if not modeled transparently.
Manufacturing and OT-adjacent connectivity increase operational risk; any migration approach that appears to threaten plant continuity or controlled integration boundaries will be penalized.
Confidentiality restrictions may constrain use of partners, tooling, offshore teams, or hyperscaler solution reviews unless consent and information-handling controls are clearly addressed.
